text.title: RASA Framework, Listening Levels, and Reflective Listening
text.summary: The RASA model (Receive, Appreciate, Summarize, Ask), the five empathic listening levels, and the reflective listening formula for communicating both content and emotional understanding.

rule.statement: Work through all four RASA steps in every substantive conversation: Receive (full attention), Appreciate (acknowledgment), Summarize (reflect back), Ask (open follow-up).
rule.rationale: Skipping Summarize is the most common failure — without paraphrasing, the speaker cannot confirm their message was understood and will either repeat or withhold detail.

example.note: Requirements session with a skeptical stakeholder.
example.code: R: No phone, eye contact, note-taking only. A: "I see", nods, "Go on..." S: "So if I understand you, the current process takes 4 hours because the data export step is manual — is that right?" A: "Can you walk me through what happens after the export?"

rule.statement: Use What, How, and Tell-me-about openers instead of Is, Do, or Did — closed questions produce yes/no answers that close down exploration.
rule.rationale: Open questions give the speaker control of the answer scope, which surfaces information the interviewer did not know to ask about.

example.code: "Is the system slow?" "Do you like the design?" "Did that cause a problem?"
"How does the system perform under load?" "What do you think about the design?" "How did that affect your team?"

rule.statement: Target Level 5 (Empathic) in high-stakes conversations: understand both the content of what is said and the emotional state of the speaker.
rule.rationale: Levels 1-4 process words; Level 5 processes meaning and feeling. Decision-makers and clients operating under stress communicate 40-60% of their actual concern through emotional register, not explicit words.

list.item: Level 1 — Ignoring: not listening at all
Level 3 — Selective: hearing only parts while forming a reply
Level 4 — Attentive: focusing on words, taking notes
Level 5 — Empathic: understanding feeling AND content

rule.statement: Reflect both content and feeling using the formula: "It sounds like you're [FEELING] because [CONTENT]."
rule.rationale: Naming the feeling signals that you are operating at Level 5; the speaker either confirms (and feels understood) or corrects (providing higher-quality signal).

example.code: "It sounds like you're frustrated because the deadline keeps moving." "It seems like you're excited about this new approach." "I hear that you're concerned about the budget implications."

rule.statement: Mirror by repeating the last 1-3 words of the speaker's sentence as a question — do not add interpretation.
rule.rationale: Mirroring is the lowest-friction technique that produces the highest elaboration rate. It signals attention without leading the speaker toward a particular answer.

example.code: Speaker: "We're really struggling with the deployment process." You: "The deployment process?" Speaker: [elaborates with root cause and context]
